summary:
Wide divergent root configurations can lead to incomplete resolution due to slow resorption processes; this increases risk of tooth fragments remaining after exfoliation. Furthermore, such anatomical features are more prone to breakage making their safe extraction challenging before full maturation is achieved.
To address your query directly:

The main issue highlighted by Agent1 concerning "Wide Divergent Roots" involves potential incompleteness post-extraction owing to prolonged resorption periods which may result in retained parts within the jawbone. Also critical is heightened susceptibility towards fracturing when attempting premature extractions from immature dentitions characterized by wider divergence angles between pulp horns compared typical narrower ones found among other types of primary molars.
In summary,
- Incomplete resolution often occurs as a consequence of extended resorptive phases characteristic of wide divergent roots leading sometimes to fragment retention inside alveolar sockets even upon initial shedding events;
- Fragile nature predisposes affected individuals toward accidental breaks particularly if intervention happens prematurely relative to natural physiological timelines governing complete loss via Furthermore, such anatomical features are more prone to breakage making their safe extraction challenging before full maturation is achieved.
